quiz_questions.question_text: How do ionic bonds form?

options.text: Through the transfer of electrons between atoms
By sharing electrons equally
Through nuclear fusion
By absorbing photons

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic bonds form when one atom transfers electrons to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other through electrostatic force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to electrons during ionic bond formation?

options.text: They are shared equally
They are transferred from metal to nonmetal
They are destroyed
They become neutrons

quiz_questions.explanation: During ionic bond formation, electrons are transferred from the metal atom (which becomes a cation) to the nonmetal atom (which becomes an anion).

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of elements typically form ionic bonds together?

options.text: Two nonmetals
Two metals
A metal and a nonmetal
Two noble gases

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic bonds typically form between a metal (which loses electrons easily) and a nonmetal (which gains electrons easily).

quiz_questions.question_text: What force holds ions together in an ionic bond?

options.text: Gravitational force
Nuclear force
Electrostatic attraction
Magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: Electrostatic attraction, the force between opposite charges, holds the positively charged cation and negatively charged anion together in an ionic bond.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which property is characteristic of ionic compounds?

options.text: Low boiling points
High melting points
Soft and flexible texture
Poor conductivity when dissolved

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic compounds have high melting points because the strong electrostatic attractions between ions in the crystal lattice require a large amount of energy to overcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when sodium reacts with chlorine?

options.text: They share electrons equally
Chlorine transfers an electron to sodium
Sodium transfers one electron to chlorine, forming Na+ and Cl-
Nothing happens because they are both stable

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium transfers its one valence electron to chlorine, forming Na+ (cation) and Cl- (anion). These oppositely charged ions attract to form NaCl.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are ionic compounds brittle?

options.text: They have weak bonds
Like charges repel when layers shift, causing the crystal to shatter
They are made of gases
They lack electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: When an ionic crystal is struck, layers of ions shift. Like-charged ions end up next to each other, causing strong repulsions that shatter the crystal.

quiz_questions.question_text: When do ionic compounds conduct electricity?

options.text: Only as solids
When dissolved in water or melted
Never
Only at low temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when dissolved in water or melted because the ions become free to move and carry charge. As solids, the ions are fixed in the lattice.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the electron configuration of Na+ after bond formation?

options.text: Same as argon
Same as sodium
Same as neon (1s2 2s2 2p6)
Same as helium

quiz_questions.explanation: When sodium loses its one valence electron, Na+ has 10 electrons arranged like neon (1s2 2s2 2p6), achieving a stable noble gas configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to the size of a metal atom when it becomes a cation?

options.text: It increases
It decreases
It stays the same
It doubles

quiz_questions.explanation: When a metal atom loses electrons to become a cation, it becomes smaller because there are fewer electrons but the same nuclear charge, pulling the remaining electrons closer.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a crystal lattice?

options.text: A repeating three-dimensional arrangement of ions in an ionic compound
A single molecule of an ionic compound
A type of covalent bond network
A metallic bonding structure

quiz_questions.explanation: A crystal lattice is the orderly, repeating three-dimensional pattern in which cations and anions are arranged in an ionic solid, maximizing attractive forces between opposite charges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the octet rule?

options.text: The tendency of atoms to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons
A rule stating all atoms must have exactly eight total electrons
A rule that atoms always form eight bonds
A requirement that elements must be in Group 8

quiz_questions.explanation: The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a stable configuration of eight valence electrons, similar to noble gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is lattice energy?

options.text: The energy to break a covalent bond
The energy released when gaseous ions come together to form a solid ionic compound
The kinetic energy of ions in solution
The energy of a single electron in an orbital

quiz_questions.explanation: Lattice energy is the energy released when separated gaseous ions form a solid ionic crystal. Higher lattice energy means a more stable and harder-to-melt compound.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an electrolyte?

options.text: A substance that conducts electricity when dissolved in water due to free-moving ions
A type of battery
A pure metal conductor
An insulating material

quiz_questions.explanation: An electrolyte is a substance that dissociates into ions when dissolved in water, allowing the solution to conduct electricity. Ionic compounds are common electrolytes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electron transfer?

options.text: The movement of one or more electrons from one atom to another during ionic bond formation
The sharing of electrons between two atoms
The removal of electrons from the nucleus
The flow of electrons in a wire

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron transfer is the process in which one or more electrons move from one atom (typically a metal) to another atom (typically a nonmetal), forming ions and an ionic bond.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a coordination number in a crystal lattice?

options.text: The number of nearest-neighbor ions surrounding a given ion in a crystal
The total number of ions in the compound
The charge of the central ion
The atomic number of the metal

quiz_questions.explanation: The coordination number is the count of nearest-neighbor ions surrounding a given ion in a crystal lattice. In NaCl, each Na+ is surrounded by 6 Cl- ions, giving a coordination number of 6.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ionization energy?

options.text: The energy needed to remove an electron from a gaseous atom
The energy released when an ion forms a bond
The energy stored in the nucleus
The energy of an electron in the ground state

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionization energy is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom. Metals have low ionization energies, making it easier for them to form cations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electron affinity?

options.text: The energy change when an atom gains an electron
The attraction between electrons and protons in the same atom
The number of electrons an atom can hold
The speed at which electrons orbit the nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron affinity is the energy change that occurs when a gaseous atom gains an electron. Nonmetals like halogens have large negative electron affinities, meaning they readily accept electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is dissociation in the context of ionic compounds?

options.text: The separation of an ionic compound into its individual ions when dissolved in water
The breaking of covalent bonds in a molecule
The evaporation of a solid compound
The nuclear decay of an atom

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissociation is the process by which an ionic compound separates into its constituent cations and anions when dissolved in water or melted, allowing ion movement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electronegativity?

options.text: The ability of an atom to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond
The charge of an ion
The number of electrons in the valence shell
The size of an atom

quiz_questions.explanation: Electronegativity measures how strongly an atom attracts electrons in a bond. A large electronegativity difference between two atoms (greater than 1.7) typically indicates ionic bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student mixes sodium metal with chlorine gas in a lab demonstration. She observes a bright flash and a white crystalline solid forms. What type of bonding occurred?

options.text: Covalent bonding through electron sharing
Ionic bonding through electron transfer from sodium to chlorine
Metallic bonding between the two elements
No bonding occurred; it was just a physical change

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium (a metal) transfers its valence electron to chlorine (a nonmetal), forming Na+ and Cl- ions. The energy release from the ionic bond formation causes the bright flash.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher places solid NaCl in an electrical circuit and the bulb does not light. She then dissolves the NaCl in water and the bulb lights up. Why?

options.text: Solid NaCl has ions locked in place; dissolved NaCl has free-moving ions that conduct electricity
Water creates new ions that were not in the solid
The solid lost its charge during the experiment
Water conducts electricity on its own without the salt

quiz_questions.explanation: In solid NaCl, ions are fixed in the crystal lattice and cannot move. When dissolved, the ions dissociate and become free to move through the solution, carrying electrical current.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student drops an ionic crystal on a hard surface and it shatters into pieces. Her lab partner asks why it broke instead of bending. What is the correct explanation?

options.text: Ionic compounds have weak bonds that break easily
When layers shift, like-charged ions align and repel, causing the crystal to fracture
The crystal was already cracked before it fell
Ionic compounds are always in a gaseous state and just appear solid

quiz_questions.explanation: When force shifts a layer of ions, cations end up next to cations and anions next to anions. The resulting repulsion between like charges causes the crystal to crack and shatter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher compares the melting points of NaCl (801 degrees C) and MgO (2852 degrees C). Both are ionic compounds, so why does MgO melt at a much higher temperature?

options.text: MgO has more atoms per formula unit
MgO has higher charges (+2 and -2 vs +1 and -1) and smaller ions, giving stronger ionic bonds
MgO is a covalent compound, not ionic
NaCl dissolves before it melts

quiz_questions.explanation: MgO has ions with +2 and -2 charges compared to NaCl's +1 and -1 charges. Higher charges and smaller ionic radii mean stronger electrostatic attraction and higher lattice energy, requiring more energy to melt.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student examines a white solid and finds it dissolves in water, conducts electricity in solution, and has a high melting point. She concludes it is ionic. Which observation is most important for identifying ionic bonding?

options.text: Its white color
Its ability to conduct electricity when dissolved, indicating free-moving ions
Its ability to dissolve in water
Its solid state at room temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Electrical conductivity in solution directly indicates the presence of free ions, which is the hallmark of ionic compounds. Many non-ionic substances also dissolve in water or are white solids.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a class activity, students model the formation of lithium fluoride. Lithium has 1 valence electron and fluorine has 7. What happens to the electron during bond formation?

options.text: Lithium's valence electron is transferred to fluorine, giving both noble gas configurations
Fluorine's electrons are transferred to lithium
Both atoms share the electron equally
The electron is destroyed during the reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: Lithium transfers its 1 valence electron to fluorine. Lithium achieves a helium configuration (2 electrons), and fluorine achieves a neon configuration (10 electrons), forming Li+ and F-.

quiz_questions.question_text: A water treatment plant uses ionic compounds to purify water. When calcium chloride (CaCl2) dissolves, how many total ions does each formula unit produce?

options.text: 2 ions (one Ca and one Cl)
3 ions (one Ca2+ and two Cl-)
4 ions (two Ca and two Cl)
1 ion (the entire compound stays together)

quiz_questions.explanation: When CaCl2 dissolves, it dissociates into one Ca2+ ion and two Cl- ions, producing a total of 3 free ions per formula unit.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked to predict whether magnesium and oxygen will form an ionic bond. Magnesium has an electronegativity of 1.31 and oxygen has 3.44. What should the student conclude?

options.text: They will form a covalent bond because the difference is small
They will form an ionic bond because the electronegativity difference (2.13) is greater than 1.7
They cannot form any bond
They will form a metallic bond

quiz_questions.explanation: The electronegativity difference is 3.44 - 1.31 = 2.13, which is greater than 1.7. This large difference indicates electron transfer and ionic bond formation.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a chemistry lab, a student heats solid potassium bromide (KBr) until it melts and then tests its conductivity. She finds the molten KBr conducts electricity. Why?

options.text: Heat created new electrons in the compound
Melting freed the K+ and Br- ions to move and carry charge
The compound converted to a metal when heated
Potassium bromide is actually a covalent compound

quiz_questions.explanation: When KBr melts, the crystal lattice breaks apart and the K+ and Br- ions become free to move. Mobile ions can carry electric current, so molten ionic compounds conduct electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A materials scientist needs a compound with very high lattice energy for a heat-resistant ceramic. She is choosing between NaF and MgO. Which should she choose and why?

options.text: NaF, because it has more electrons
MgO, because its ions have higher charges and smaller radii, giving greater lattice energy
NaF, because sodium is more reactive
Both have the same lattice energy since they are 1:1 compounds

quiz_questions.explanation: MgO has Mg2+ and O2- (charges of 2+/2-) compared to NaF's Na+ and F- (charges of 1+/1-). Higher ion charges and smaller sizes produce much greater lattice energy and higher melting points.
